([/rɛnsəˈlɪər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*ren-sə-LEER*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); **RPI**), a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university")  [research-university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Troy, New York](/wiki/Troy,_New_York "Troy, New York"), United States, is the oldest [technological university](/wiki/Technological_university "Technological university") in the [English-speaking world](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world") and in the Western Hemisphere. [Stephen Van Rensselaer](/wiki/Stephen_Van_Rensselaer "Stephen Van Rensselaer") and [Amos Eaton](/wiki/Amos_Eaton "Amos Eaton") established RPI in 1824 for the "application of science to the common purposes of life".[[7]](#cite_note-history-7)

**Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute** ([/rɛnsəˈlɪər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*ren-sə-LEER*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); **RPI**), a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university")  [research-university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Troy, New York](/wiki/Troy,_New_York "Troy, New York"), United States, is the oldest [technological university](/wiki/Technological_university "Technological university") in the [English-speaking world](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world") and in the Western Hemisphere. [Stephen Van Rensselaer](/wiki/Stephen_Van_Rensselaer "Stephen Van Rensselaer") and [Amos Eaton](/wiki/Amos_Eaton "Amos Eaton") established RPI in 1824 for the "application of science to the common purposes of life".[[7]](#cite_note-history-7)

Built on a hillside, RPI's 265-acre (107 ha) campus overlooks the city of [Troy](/wiki/Troy,_New_York "Troy, New York") and the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"). The institute operates an on‑campus [business incubator](/wiki/Business_incubator "Business incubator") and the 1,250-acre (510 ha) [Rensselaer Technology Park](/wiki/Rensselaer_Technology_Park "Rensselaer Technology Park").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Organizationally, RPI has six main schools which contain 37 departments, with an emphasis on science and technology.[[9]](#cite_note-9) It is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities: Very High Research Activity".[[10]](#cite_note-Carnegie-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute](/wiki/History_of_Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute "History of Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute")

### 1824–1900

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1824–1900")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/StephenVanRensselaerIIIPortrait.jpg/250px-StephenVanRensselaerIIIPortrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:StephenVanRensselaerIIIPortrait.jpg)

[Stephen van Rensselaer](/wiki/Stephen_van_Rensselaer_III "Stephen van Rensselaer III"), founder of Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Rensselaer_School_1824.png/250px-Rensselaer_School_1824.png)](/wiki/File:Rensselaer_School_1824.png)

Engraving of the original Rensselaer School

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/RPI_Engraving_1879.jpg/250px-RPI_Engraving_1879.jpg)](/wiki/File:RPI_Engraving_1879.jpg)

Engraving of RPI in 1876

[Stephen Van Rensselaer](/wiki/Stephen_Van_Rensselaer "Stephen Van Rensselaer") established the Rensselaer School on 5 November 1824 with a letter to the Reverend Dr. [Samuel Blatchford](/wiki/Samuel_Blatchford_(university_president) "Samuel Blatchford (university president)"), in which Van Rensselaer asked Blatchford to serve as the first president. Within the letter he set down several orders of business. He appointed [Amos Eaton](/wiki/Amos_Eaton "Amos Eaton") as the school's first senior professor and appointed the first board of trustees.[[11]](#cite_note-historybook-11) The school opened on Monday, 5 January 1825 at the Old Bank Place, a building at the north end of Troy.[[12]](#cite_note-buildings-12) Tuition was around $40 per semester (equivalent to $800 in 2012[[13]](#cite_note-13)).[[11]](#cite_note-historybook-11) The fact that the school attracted students from as far as [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") is attributed to the reputation of Eaton. Fourteen months of successful trial led to the incorporation of the school on 21 March 1826 by the state of [New York](/wiki/New_York_State "New York State"). In its early years, the Rensselaer School strongly resembled a graduate school more than it did a college, drawing graduates from many older institutions.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Under Eaton, the Rensselaer School, renamed the Rensselaer Institute in 1832, was a small but vibrant center for technological research. The first civil engineering degrees in the [United States](/wiki/United_States_of_America "United States of America") were granted by the school in 1835, and many of the best remembered civil engineers of that time graduated from the school. Important visiting scholars included [Joseph Henry](/wiki/Joseph_Henry "Joseph Henry"), who had previously studied under Amos Eaton, and [Thomas Davenport](/wiki/Thomas_Davenport_(inventor) "Thomas Davenport (inventor)"), who sold the world's first working [electric motor](/wiki/Electric_motor "Electric motor") to the institute.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 1847 alumnus [Benjamin Franklin Greene](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin_Greene "Benjamin Franklin Greene") became the new senior professor. Earlier he had done a thorough study of European technical schools to see how Rensselaer could be improved. In 1850 he reorganized the school into a three-year polytechnic institute with six technical schools.[[16]](#cite_note-timeline-16) In 1861 the name was changed to Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute.[[17]](#cite_note-17) A severe conflagration of 10 May 1862, known as "[The Great Fire](/wiki/Great_Fire_of_Troy "Great Fire of Troy")", destroyed more than 507 buildings in Troy and gutted 75 acres (300,000 m2) in the heart of the city.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-great_fire-19) The "Infant School" building that housed the institute at the time was destroyed in this fire. [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") proposed that Rensselaer leave Troy altogether and merge with its New York City campus. Ultimately, the proposal was rejected, and the campus left the crowded downtown for the hillside. Classes were temporarily held at the Vail House and in the [Troy University](/wiki/Troy_University_(New_York) "Troy University (New York)") building until 1864,[[20]](#cite_note-20) when the institute moved to a building on Broadway on 8th Street, now the site of the Approach.[[19]](#cite_note-great_fire-19)

One of the first Latino student organizations in the United States was founded at RPI in 1890. The [Club Hispano Americano](/wiki/Club_Hispano_Americano "Club Hispano Americano") was established by the international Latin American students that attended the institute at this time.

### Since 1900

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Since 1900")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Rensselaer_poly_coat_arms_1904.svg/250px-Rensselaer_poly_coat_arms_1904.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Rensselaer_poly_coat_arms_1904.svg)

Seal with the coat of arms designed by [Richard Clipston Sturgis](/wiki/Richard_Clipston_Sturgis "Richard Clipston Sturgis") in 1904[[11]](#cite_note-historybook-11)

In 1904, the institute was for the fourth time devastated by fire, when its main building was completely destroyed.[[21]](#cite_note-21) However, RPI underwent a period of academic and resource expansion under the leadership of President [Palmer Ricketts](/wiki/Palmer_C._Ricketts "Palmer C. Ricketts").[[22]](#cite_note-22) Named president in 1901, Ricketts liberalized the curriculum by adding the Department of Arts, Science, and Business Administration, in addition to the Graduate School. He also expanded the university's resources and developed RPI into a true polytechnic institute by increasing the number of degrees offered from two to twelve; these included electrical engineering, mechanical engineering, biology, chemistry, and physics. During Rickett's tenure, enrollment increased from approximately 200 in 1900 to a high of 1,700 in 1930.[[23]](#cite_note-railroad_guide-23)

Another period of expansion occurred following [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") as returning veterans used their GI Bill education benefits to attend college. The "Freshman Hill" residence complex was opened in 1953 followed by the completion of the Commons Dining Hall in 1954, two more halls in 1958, and three more in 1968. In this same time frame (1966) [Herta Regina Leng](/wiki/Herta_Regina_Leng "Herta Regina Leng") was appointed as RPI's first female full professor. She is now honored there with an annual lecture series.[[24]](#cite_note-24) In 1961, there was major progress in academics at the institute with the construction of the Gaerttner [Linear Accelerator](/wiki/Linear_particle_accelerator "Linear particle accelerator"), then the most powerful in the world,[[25]](#cite_note-25) and the Jonsson-Rowland Science Center. The current Student Union building was opened in 1967.

The next three decades brought continued growth with many new buildings (see '[Campus'](#Campus) below), and growing ties to industry. The "H-building", previously used for storage, became the home for the RPI incubator program, the first such program sponsored solely by a university.[[26]](#cite_note-first_incubator-26) Shortly after this, RPI decided to invest $3 million in pavement, water and power on around 1,200 acres (490 ha) of land it owned 5 miles (8.0 km) south of campus to create the [Rensselaer Technology Park](/wiki/Rensselaer_Technology_Park "Rensselaer Technology Park").[[27]](#cite_note-techpark-27) In 1982 the New York State legislature granted RPI $30 million to build the [George M. Low Center for Industrial Innovation](/wiki/George_M._Low_Center_for_Industrial_Innovation "George M. Low Center for Industrial Innovation"), a center for industry-sponsored research and development.

In 1999, RPI gained attention when it was one of the first universities to implement a mandatory [laptop computer](/wiki/Laptop_computer "Laptop computer") program.[[28]](#cite_note-28) This was also the year of the arrival of [Shirley Ann Jackson](/wiki/Shirley_Ann_Jackson "Shirley Ann Jackson"), a former chairperson of the [Nuclear Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Nuclear_Regulatory_Commission "Nuclear Regulatory Commission") under U.S. [President Bill Clinton](/wiki/President_Bill_Clinton "President Bill Clinton"), as the eighteenth president of RPI. She instituted "[The Rensselaer Plan](/wiki/Rensselaer_Plan "Rensselaer Plan")" (discussed below), an ambitious plan to revitalize the institute. Many advances have been made under the plan, and Jackson has enjoyed the ongoing support of the RPI Board of Trustees. However, her leadership style did not sit well with many faculty;[[29]](#cite_note-chronicle_president-29) on 26 April 2006, RPI faculty voted 149 to 155 in a failed vote of no-confidence in Jackson.[[30]](#cite_note-30) In September 2007, RPI's Faculty Senate was suspended for over four years over conflict with the administration.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-AAUP_demise-32) In 2011, the college was [sanctioned](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_sanctioned_by_the_American_Association_of_University_Professors "List of colleges and universities sanctioned by the American Association of University Professors") by the [American Association of University Professors](/wiki/American_Association_of_University_Professors "American Association of University Professors") "for infringement of governance standards".[[33]](#cite_note-33)

On 3 October 2008, RPI celebrated the opening of the $220 million [Experimental Media and Performing Arts Center](/wiki/Experimental_Media_and_Performing_Arts_Center "Experimental Media and Performing Arts Center"). That same year the national economic downturn resulted in the elimination of 98 staff positions across the institute, about five percent of the workforce.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Campus construction expansion continued, however, with the completion of the $92 million East Campus Athletic Village and opening of the new Blitman Commons residence hall in 2009. As of 2015, all staff positions had been reinstated at the institute, experiencing significant growth from pre-recession levels and contributing over $1 billion annually to the economy of the [Capital District](/wiki/Capital_District,_New_York "Capital District, New York").[[35]](#cite_note-35) That same year, renovation of the North Hall, E-Complex, and Quadrangle dormitories began and was later completed in 2016 to house one of the largest incoming classes in Rensselaer's history.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) In 2022, Rensselaer hosted a incoming class size of 2,110 students, the largest class size in Rensselaer's history.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In July 2022, [Martin A. Schmidt](/wiki/Martin_A._Schmidt "Martin A. Schmidt"), formerly provost of [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology"), became RPI's nineteenth president.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Campus")]

RPI's 275-acre (111 ha)[[40]](#cite_note-virtual_tour-40) campus sits upon a hill overlooking [Troy](/wiki/Troy,_New_York "Troy, New York") and the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"). The surrounding area is mostly residential neighborhoods, with the city of Troy lying at the base of the hill. The campus is bisected by 15th Street, with most of the athletic and housing facilities to the east, and the academic buildings to the west. A footbridge spans the street, linking the two halves. Much of the campus features a series of [Colonial Revival style](/wiki/Colonial_Revival_style "Colonial Revival style") structures built in the first three decades of the 20th century. Overall, the campus has enjoyed four periods of expansion.[[12]](#cite_note-buildings-12)

### 1824–1905

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1824–1905")]

RPI was originally located in downtown Troy, but gradually moved to the hilltop that overlooks the city. Buildings that remain from this time include [Winslow Chemical Laboratory](/wiki/Winslow_Chemical_Laboratory "Winslow Chemical Laboratory"), a building on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"). Located at the base of the hill on the western edge of campus, it currently houses the Social and Behavioral Research Laboratory.[[12]](#cite_note-buildings-12)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### Ricketts Campus, 1906–1935

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ricketts Campus, 1906–1935")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/RusselSageLabs.JPG/250px-RusselSageLabs.JPG)](/wiki/File:RusselSageLabs.JPG)

Russell Sage Laboratory

President Palmer Ricketts supervised the construction of the school's "Green Rooftop" Colonial Revival buildings that give much of the campus a distinct architectural style. Buildings constructed during this period include the [Carnegie Building](/wiki/Carnegie_Building_(Troy,_New_York) "Carnegie Building (Troy, New York)") (1906), Walker Laboratory (1907), Russell Sage Laboratory (1909),[[42]](#cite_note-Russell_Sage_Laboratory_history-42) [Pittsburgh Building](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Building "Pittsburgh Building") (1912), Quadrangle Dormitories (1916–1927), Troy Building (1925), [Amos Eaton Hall](/wiki/Amos_Eaton_Hall "Amos Eaton Hall") (1928), [Greene Building](/wiki/Greene_Building,_Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute "Greene Building, Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute") (1931) and [Ricketts Building](/wiki/Ricketts_Building "Ricketts Building") (1935). Also built during this period was "The Approach" (1907), a massive ornate granite staircase found on the west end of campus. Originally linking RPI to the Troy Union Railroad station, it again serves as an important link between the city and the university.[[43]](#cite_note-43) In 1906 the '86 Field, home field of the football team until 2008, was completed with support of the Class of 1886. In 1912, to replace the old gymnasium, the Class of 1887 presented the '87 Gym to the campus, which featured a large swimming pool, indoor track, and bowling alleys.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Post-war expansion, 1946–1960

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Post-war expansion, 1946–1960")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/West_hall_twilight.jpg/250px-West_hall_twilight.jpg)](/wiki/File:West_hall_twilight.jpg)

[West Hall](/wiki/West_Hall_(Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute) "West Hall (Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/VorheesComputingCenter.JPG/250px-VorheesComputingCenter.JPG)](/wiki/File:VorheesComputingCenter.JPG)

The [Voorhees](/wiki/Alan_M._Voorhees "Alan M. Voorhees") Computing Center

After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the campus again underwent major expansion. Nine dormitories were built at the east edge of campus bordering Burdett Avenue, a location that came to be called "Freshman Hill". The [Houston Field House](/wiki/Houston_Field_House "Houston Field House") (1949) was reassembled, after being moved in pieces from its original [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island") location. [West Hall](/wiki/West_Hall_(Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute) "West Hall (Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute)"), which was originally built in 1869 as a hospital, was acquired by the institute in 1953. The ornate building is an example of [French Second Empire](/wiki/Second_Empire_(architecture) "Second Empire (architecture)") architecture.[[45]](#cite_note-45) It was listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 1973.

Another unique building is the Voorhees Computing Center (VCC). Originally the St. Joseph's Seminary chapel, it was built in 1933 and acquired by Rensselaer in 1958, and after renovation served as the institute's library from 1960 until the completion of the new [Folsom Library](/wiki/Folsom_Library "Folsom Library"), in 1976.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The Folsom Library, located adjacent to the computing center, has a concrete exterior that was designed to harmonize with the light gray brick of the chapel;[[47]](#cite_note-47) architecturally, it is an example of the modern [brutalist](/wiki/Brutalist_architecture "Brutalist architecture") style.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Subsequently, the university was unsure of what to do with the chapel, or whether to keep it at all, but in 1979 decided to preserve it and renovate it to house computer labs and facilities to support the institute's computing initiatives. Today the VCC serves as the backbone for the institute's data and [telephony](/wiki/Telephony "Telephony") infrastructure.

### Modern campus, since 1961

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Modern campus, since 1961")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/BiotechCenterRPI.jpg/250px-BiotechCenterRPI.jpg)](/wiki/File:BiotechCenterRPI.jpg)

[Center for Biotechnology and Interdisciplinary Studies](/wiki/Center_for_Biotechnology_and_Interdisciplinary_Studies "Center for Biotechnology and Interdisciplinary Studies")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/EMPACTwilight.JPG/250px-EMPACTwilight.JPG)](/wiki/File:EMPACTwilight.JPG)

[Experimental Media and Performing Arts Center](/wiki/Experimental_Media_and_Performing_Arts_Center "Experimental Media and Performing Arts Center")

The modern campus features the [Jonsson-Rowland Science Center](/wiki/Jonsson-Rowland_Science_Center "Jonsson-Rowland Science Center") (J-ROWL) (1961), Materials Research Center (MRC) (1965), Rensselaer Union (1967), Cogswell Laboratory (1971), Darrin Communications Center (DCC) (1973), [Jonsson Engineering Center](/wiki/Jonsson_Engineering_Center "Jonsson Engineering Center") (JEC) (1977), [Low Center for Industrial Innovation](/wiki/Low_Center_for_Industrial_Innovation "Low Center for Industrial Innovation") (CII) (1987), a public school building which was converted into Academy Hall (1990), and the [Center for Biotechnology and Interdisciplinary Studies](/wiki/Center_for_Biotechnology_and_Interdisciplinary_Studies "Center for Biotechnology and Interdisciplinary Studies") (2004).[[12]](#cite_note-buildings-12) Tunnels connect the Low Center, DCC, JEC and Science Center. A tenth dormitory named Barton Hall was added to Freshman Hill in August 2000, featuring the largest rooms available for freshmen.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

On 3 October 2008, the university celebrated the grand opening of the [Experimental Media and Performing Arts Center](/wiki/Experimental_Media_and_Performing_Arts_Center "Experimental Media and Performing Arts Center") (EMPAC) situated on the west edge of campus.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The building was constructed on the precipice of the hill, with the main entrance on top. Upon entering, elevated walkways lead into a 1,200-seat concert hall. Most of the building is encased in a glass exoskeleton, with an [atrium](/wiki/Atrium_(architecture) "Atrium (architecture)")-like space between it and the "inner building". Adjacent to and underneath the main auditorium there is a 400-seat theater, offices, and two black-box studios with 35-foot (11 m) to 45-foot (14 m) ceilings.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Originally budgeted for $50 million, the EMPAC construction costs ballooned to over $200 million due to difficulty of anchoring the foundation in the soft clay of the hill.[[52]](#cite_note-Brunelle-52)

In 2008, RPI announced the purchase of the former Rensselaer [Best Western](/wiki/Best_Western "Best Western") Inn, located at the base of the hill, along with plans to transform it into a new residence hall. After extensive renovations, the residence hall was dedicated on 15 May 2009, as the Howard N. Blitman, P.E. '50 Residence Commons.[[53]](#cite_note-bestwestern-53) It houses about 300 students in 148 rooms and includes a fitness center, dining hall, and conference area.[[53]](#cite_note-bestwestern-53) The new residence hall is part of a growing initiative to involve students in the Troy community and help revitalize the downtown. RPI owns and operates three office buildings in downtown Troy, the Rice and Heley buildings and the historic [W. & L.E. Gurley Building](/wiki/W._%26_L.E._Gurley_Building "W. & L.E. Gurley Building").[[54]](#cite_note-54) RPI also owns the [Proctor's Theater](/wiki/Proctor%27s_Theater_(Troy,_New_York) "Proctor's Theater (Troy, New York)") building in Troy, which was purchased in 2004, with the intention of converting it into office space.[[55]](#cite_note-RPI_purchase-55) As of 2011, Rensselaer had signed an agreement with Columbia Development Companies to acquire both Proctor's Theatre and Chasan Building in Troy and launch a redevelopment.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-Liz_Young_TU_blog-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-Gutting_proposal-59)

### Other campuses and online programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other campuses and online programs")]

Main article: [Rensselaer at Work](/wiki/Rensselaer_at_Work "Rensselaer at Work")

The institute ran a 15-acre (6.1 ha) campus in [Hartford, Connecticut](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut"), and a distance learning center in [Groton, Connecticut](/wiki/Groton,_Connecticut "Groton, Connecticut"), the latter of which was closed in 2018. The Hartford campus was sold in 2023.[[60]](#cite_note-60) These centers were used by graduates and working professionals and are now managed by the online branch of RPI, [Rensselaer at Work](/wiki/Rensselaer_at_Work "Rensselaer at Work") which offers graduate degrees in business, computer science, and engineering. There are also certificate programs and skills training programs for working professionals.

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Academics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/RPIAcademyHall.JPG/250px-RPIAcademyHall.JPG)](/wiki/File:RPIAcademyHall.JPG)

Academy Hall

Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute has five schools: the School of Architecture, the School of Engineering, the School of Humanities, Arts, and Social Sciences, the [Lally School of Management & Technology](/wiki/Lally_School_of_Management_%26_Technology "Lally School of Management & Technology"), and the School of Science. The School of Engineering is the largest by enrollment, followed by the School of Science, the School of Management, the School of Humanities, Arts, and Social Sciences, and the School of Architecture. There also exists an interdisciplinary program in [Information Technology](/wiki/Information_Technology "Information Technology") that began in the late 1990s, programs in prehealth and prelaw, [Reserve Officers' Training Corps](/wiki/Reserve_Officers%27_Training_Corps "Reserve Officers' Training Corps") (ROTC) for students desiring commissions as officers in the armed forces, a program in [cooperative education](/wiki/Cooperative_education "Cooperative education") (Co-Op), and domestic and international exchange programs. Altogether, the university offers over 145 programs in nearly 60 fields that lead to bachelor's, master's, and doctoral degrees. In addition to traditional majors, RPI has around a dozen special interdisciplinary programs, such as Games and Simulation Arts and Sciences (GSAS), Design, Innovation, and Society (DIS), Minds & Machines, and Product Design and Innovation (PDI).[[61]](#cite_note-61) RPI is a technology-oriented university; all buildings and residence hall rooms have hard-wired and wireless high speed internet access, and all incoming freshmen have been required to have a laptop computer since 1999.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Rensselaer Plan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rensselaer Plan")]

With the arrival of President [Shirley Ann Jackson](/wiki/Shirley_Ann_Jackson "Shirley Ann Jackson") came the "Rensselaer Plan", announced in 1999. Its goal is to achieve greater prominence for Rensselaer as a technological research university.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Various aspects of the plan include bringing in a larger [graduate student](/wiki/Graduate_student "Graduate student") population and new research faculty, and increasing participation in undergraduate research, international [exchange programs](/wiki/Student_exchange_program "Student exchange program"), and "living and learning communities". So far, there have been a number of changes under the plan: new infrastructure such as the [Center for Biotechnology and Interdisciplinary Studies](/wiki/Center_for_Biotechnology_and_Interdisciplinary_Studies "Center for Biotechnology and Interdisciplinary Studies"), [Experimental Media and Performing Arts Center](/wiki/Experimental_Media_and_Performing_Arts_Center "Experimental Media and Performing Arts Center"), and [Computational Center for Nanotechnology Innovations](/wiki/Computational_Center_for_Nanotechnology_Innovations "Computational Center for Nanotechnology Innovations") (CCNI) have been built to support new programs, and application numbers have increased.[[64]](#cite_note-accomplished-64) In 2018, Rensselaer received a record number of applications: 20,337.[[65]](#cite_note-65) According to [Jared Cohon](/wiki/Jared_Cohon "Jared Cohon") in 2006, then-president of [Carnegie Mellon University](/wiki/Carnegie_Mellon_University "Carnegie Mellon University"), "Change at Rensselaer in the last five years has occurred with a scope and swiftness that may be without precedent in the recent history of American higher education."[[66]](#cite_note-66)

The university set an initial goal of $100 million annually. Fourteen years later, in FY2013, research expenditures reached this goal. To help raise money, the university mounted a $1 billion capital campaign with a public phase that began in September 2004 and was expected to finish by 2008. In 2001, a major milestone of the campaign was the pledging of an unrestricted gift of $360 million by an anonymous donor, believed to be the largest such gift to a U.S. university at the time. The university had been a relative stranger to such generosity as the prior largest single gift was $15 million.[[67]](#cite_note-67) By September 2006, the $1 billion goal has been exceeded much in part to an in-kind contribution of software commercially valued at $513.95 million by the Partners for the Advancement of Collaborative Engineering Education (PACE).[[68]](#cite_note-Rensselaer_Mag_pace-68) In light of this, the board of trustees increased the goal of the $1 billion capital campaign to $1.4 billion by June 30, 2009. The new goal was met by October 1, 2008.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

In anticipation of RPI's 200th anniversary, an updated version called the "Rensselaer Plan 2024"[[70]](#cite_note-70) was announced in 2012.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

In 2016, Jackson announced during the Fall Town Hall Meeting that the institute was in the final stages of organizing a new capital campaign which it would launch in 2017 to meet the goals of the Rensselaer Plan 2024.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The goal of the campaign was cited as being primarily for the support of financial aid for undergraduate students and the expansion of on-campus research facilities to accommodate planned increases in doctoral and graduate enrollment. The fundraising goal of the capital campaign was $1 billion, with over $400 million raised prior to the campaign going public.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

Ambitious spending on the Rensselaer Plan has led the university into financial difficulties, with its credit rating lowered by several agencies.[[52]](#cite_note-Brunelle-52)[[74]](#cite_note-Renew_Rensselaer-74)

### Electrical and computer systems engineering at RPI

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Electrical and computer systems engineering at RPI")]

One of the major departments at RPI is the ECSE department, or Electrical and Computer Systems Engineering department. The department offers students the ability to gain bachelors, masters, and doctoral degrees in [electrical engineering](/wiki/Electrical_engineering "Electrical engineering"), or [computer systems engineering](/wiki/Computer_engineering "Computer engineering"). While the school itself was founded in 1824, electrical engineering was first introduced to Rensselaer Polytechnic in 1907[[89]](#cite_note-89) with the class of 1911, long before the invention of the first computers but twenty-eight years after the invention of the lightbulb. The department was listed as Physics and Electrical Engineering in 1924 while under President [Palmer Ricketts](/wiki/Palmer_C._Ricketts "Palmer C. Ricketts"). Computer system engineering was added later. Today computer systems engineering and electrical engineering are in many ways similar, with many of the core classes being shared between the two. Electrical engineering, however tends to focus more on hardware, while computer systems engineering tends to focus on algorithms and systems. Many students take dual majors, the common ones being electrical engineering and computer systems engineering, electrical engineering and mechanic engineering, electrical engineering and applied physics, and computer systems engineering and computer science. Many students in the ECSE department often do minors in other fields such as [economics](/wiki/Economics "Economics") or [psychology](/wiki/Psychology "Psychology").[[90]](#cite_note-90) The ECSE department currently does research in the areas of information science and systems, communication and network, control and autonomy, energy and power systems, electronics and photonics, and computer systems design.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Notable alumni from the ECSE department include James A. Parsons, [B. Jayant Baliga](/wiki/B._Jayant_Baliga "B. Jayant Baliga"), Alan Borck, founder of RLC Electronics, Bruce Carlson, Mukesh Chatter, [Allen B. Du Mont](/wiki/Allen_B._DuMont "Allen B. DuMont"), [Nariman Farvardin](/wiki/Nariman_Farvardin "Nariman Farvardin"), Peter Hart, inventor of the A\* search algorithm, [Herman A. Haus](/wiki/Hermann_A._Haus "Hermann A. Haus"), [Marcian E. Hoff](/wiki/Marcian_Hoff "Marcian Hoff"), creator of the microprocessor, Doug Mercer, who was influential in the field of analog to digital converters, [Sean O’Sullivan](/wiki/Sean_O%27Sullivan_(engineer) "Sean O'Sullivan (engineer)"), [Curtis P. Priem](/wiki/Curtis_Priem "Curtis Priem"), founder of [NVIDIA](/wiki/Nvidia "Nvidia"), George Saridis, [Steven J. Sassoon](/wiki/Steven_Sasson "Steven Sasson"), inventor of digital camera, [Chauncey Starr](/wiki/Chauncey_Starr "Chauncey Starr"), [Raymond S. Tomlinson](/wiki/Ray_Tomlinson "Ray Tomlinson"), inventor of [e-mail](/wiki/Email "Email"), and Eitan Yudilevich.[[92]](#cite_note-92)
