[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/LowCenterRPI.JPG/250px-LowCenterRPI.JPG)](/wiki/File:LowCenterRPI.JPG)

The [Low Center for Industrial Innovation](/wiki/Low_Center_for_Industrial_Innovation "Low Center for Industrial Innovation"), northeast hub of the [Smart Manufacturing Innovation Institute](/wiki/National_Network_for_Manufacturing_Innovation "National Network for Manufacturing Innovation")

Rensselaer is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "[R1: Doctoral Universities – Very High Research Activity"](/wiki/List_of_research_universities_in_the_United_States#Universities_classified_as_"R1:_Doctoral_Universities_–_Very_high_research_activity" "List of research universities in the United States").[[93]](#cite_note-93) Rensselaer has established six areas of research as institute priorities: [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), [energy and the environment](/wiki/Energy_security "Energy security"), [nanotechnology](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology"), [computation and information technology](/wiki/Information_Technology "Information Technology"), and [media and the arts](/wiki/Media_studies "Media studies").[[94]](#cite_note-94) Research is organized under the Office of the Vice President for Research, [Jonathan Dordick](/wiki/Jonathan_Dordick "Jonathan Dordick").[[95]](#cite_note-95) In 2018, Rensselaer operated 34 research centers and maintained annual sponsored research expenditures of $100.8 million.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Biotech_Center_RPI.jpg/250px-Biotech_Center_RPI.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biotech_Center_RPI.jpg)

[Center for Biotechnology and Interdisciplinary Studies](/wiki/Center_for_Biotechnology_and_Interdisciplinary_Studies "Center for Biotechnology and Interdisciplinary Studies")

One of the most recent of Rensselaer's research centers is the [Center for Biotechnology and Interdisciplinary Studies](/wiki/Center_for_Biotechnology_and_Interdisciplinary_Studies "Center for Biotechnology and Interdisciplinary Studies"), a 218,000 square-foot research facility for fundamental and applied research in [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"). The primary target of the research center is biologics, a research priority based on data-driven understanding of [proteomics](/wiki/Proteomics "Proteomics"), protein regulation, and gene regulation. It involves using [biocatalysis](/wiki/Biocatalysis "Biocatalysis") and [synthetic biology](/wiki/Synthetic_biology "Synthetic biology") tools to block or supplement the actions of specific cells or proteins in the immune system. Over the past decade, CBIS has produced over 2,000 peer-reviewed publications with over 30,000 citations and currently employs over 200 scientists and engineers. The center is used primarily to train undergraduate and graduate students, with over 1,000 undergraduates and 200 doctoral students trained.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98) The center has numerous academic and industry partners, including the [Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai](/wiki/Icahn_School_of_Medicine_at_Mount_Sinai "Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai"). These partnerships have resulted in numerous advances over the last decade through new commercial developments in diagnostics, therapeutics, medical devices, and regenerative medicine which are a direct result of research at the center. Examples of advancements include the creation of synthetic [heparin](/wiki/Heparin "Heparin"), antimicrobial coatings, detoxification [chemotherapy](/wiki/Chemotherapy "Chemotherapy"), on-demand [biomedicine](/wiki/Biomedicine "Biomedicine"), implantable sensors, and 3D cellular array chips.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/IBM_Blue_Gene_P_supercomputer.jpg/250px-IBM_Blue_Gene_P_supercomputer.jpg)](/wiki/File:IBM_Blue_Gene_P_supercomputer.jpg)

A Blue Gene/P [supercomputer](/wiki/Supercomputer "Supercomputer") similar to Rensselaer's [Center for Computational Innovations](/wiki/Center_for_Computational_Innovations "Center for Computational Innovations")

Rensselaer also hosts the [Tetherless World Constellation](/wiki/Tetherless_World_Constellation "Tetherless World Constellation"), a multidisciplinary research institution focused on theories, methods, and applications of the [World Wide Web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web"). Research is carried out in three inter-connected themes: Future Web, Semantic Foundations and Xinformatics. At Rensselaer, a constellation is a multidisciplinary team composed of senior and junior faculty members, research scientists, and postdoctoral, graduate, and undergraduate students. The faculty experts for the TWC constellation are [James Hendler](/wiki/James_Hendler "James Hendler"), [Deborah McGuinness](/wiki/Deborah_McGuinness "Deborah McGuinness") and [Peter Fox](/wiki/Peter_Fox_(professor) "Peter Fox (professor)"). Faculty alumni of TWC includes [Heng Ji](/wiki/Heng_Ji "Heng Ji") ([Natural Language Processing](/wiki/Natural_Language_Processing "Natural Language Processing")). In 2016, the Constellation received a one million dollar grant from the [Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation](/wiki/Bill_%26_Melinda_Gates_Foundation "Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation") for continuing work on a novel data visualization platform that will harness and accelerate the analysis of vast amounts of data for the foundation's Healthy Birth, Growth, and Development Knowledge Integration initiative.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

In conjunction with the constellation, Rensselaer operates the [Center for Computational Innovations](/wiki/Center_for_Computational_Innovations "Center for Computational Innovations") which is the result of a $100 million collaboration between Rensselaer, [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"), and [New York State](/wiki/New_York_State "New York State") to further nanotechnology innovations. The center is currently home to the most powerful private-university based [supercomputer](/wiki/Supercomputer "Supercomputer") in the world and its supercomputer is consistently ranked among the most powerful in the world, capable of performing over 1.1 [peta-](/wiki/Peta- "Peta-")[FLOPS](/wiki/FLOPS "FLOPS"). The center's main focus is on reducing the cost associated with the development of [nanoscale](/wiki/Nanoscale "Nanoscale") materials and devices, such as those used in the [semiconductor](/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor") industry. The university also utilizes the center for [interdisciplinary](/wiki/Interdisciplinarity "Interdisciplinarity") research in [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine"), [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy"), and other fields.[[101]](#cite_note-101) Rensselaer operates a [nuclear reactor](/wiki/Nuclear_reactor "Nuclear reactor") and testing facility–the only university-run reactor in New York State–as well as the Gaerttner [Linear Accelerator](/wiki/Linear_Accelerator "Linear Accelerator"), which is currently being upgraded under a $9.44 million grant from the [US Department of Energy](/wiki/US_Department_of_Energy "US Department of Energy").[[102]](#cite_note-102)

In 2024, Rensselaer, in partnership with [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM"), unveiled a new [quantum computer](/wiki/Quantum_computer "Quantum computer") on campus, aiming to further quantum computer research for both the university and the New York State area.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

Starting December 15th, 2024, Rensselaer unveiled their new intellectual property policy,[[104]](#cite_note-104) which granted more freedoms to undergraduates and researchers who use Rensselaer facilities to retain ownership of their work. This came after professor Chulsung Bae sued the school, citing that they licensed his patents to another business.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

## Student life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Student life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/RPIStudentUnion.jpg/330px-RPIStudentUnion.jpg)](/wiki/File:RPIStudentUnion.jpg)

Student Union

The students of RPI have created and participate in a variety of clubs and organizations funded by the Student Union. About 170 of these organizations are funded by the Student Union, while another thirty, which consist mostly of political and religious organizations, are self-supporting.[[106]](#cite_note-106) In 2006 the *[Princeton Review](/wiki/The_Princeton_Review "The Princeton Review")* ranked RPI second for "more to do on campus."[[107]](#cite_note-107) The Union was the last entirely student-run union at a private university in the United States until September 2017.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

### Student organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Student organizations")]

**Phalanx** is RPI's Senior Honor Society.[[109]](#cite_note-109) It was founded in 1912, when Edward Dion and the Student Council organized a society to recognize those RPI students who had distinguished themselves among their peers in the areas of leadership, service and devotion to the *[alma mater](/wiki/Alma_mater "Alma mater")*. It is a fellowship of the most active in student activities and has inducted more than 1,500 members since its founding.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

RPI has around twenty [intramural](/wiki/Intramural_sports "Intramural sports") sports organizations, many of which are broken down into different divisions based on level of play. Greek organizations compete in them as well as independent athletes. There are also thirty-nine club sports.

Given the university's proximity to the [Berkshires](/wiki/Berkshires "Berkshires"), [Green Mountains](/wiki/Green_Mountains "Green Mountains") and [Adirondacks](/wiki/Adirondack_Mountains "Adirondack Mountains"), the Ski Club and the Outing Club are some of the largest groups on campus. The Ski Club offers weekly trips to local ski areas during the winter months,[[111]](#cite_note-111) while the Outing Club offers trips on a weekly basis for a variety of activities.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

*[The Rensselaer Polytechnic](/wiki/The_Rensselaer_Polytechnic "The Rensselaer Polytechnic")* is the student-run weekly newspaper.[[113]](#cite_note-113) *The Poly* printed about 7,000 copies each week and distributed them around campus until 2018 when the newspaper switched to online-only distribution due to budget concerns. Although it is the Union club with the largest budget,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] *The Poly* receives no subsidy from the Union and obtains all funding through the sale of advertisements. There is also a popular student-run magazine called *Statler & Waldorf* which prints on a semesterly basis.[[114]](#cite_note-SW-114)

RPI has an improvisational comedy group, Sheer Idiocy, which performs several shows a semester.[[115]](#cite_note-115) There are also several music groups ranging from *[a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella")* groups such as the rusty pipes, Partial Credit, the Rensselyrics and Duly Noted,[[116]](#cite_note-116) to several instrumental groups such as the orchestra, the jazz band and a classical choral group, the Rensselaer Concert Choir.

Another notable organization on campus is [WRPI](/wiki/WRPI "WRPI"), the campus radio station. WRPI differs from most [college radio](/wiki/College_radio "College radio") in that it serves a 75-mile (121 km) radius[[117]](#cite_note-117) including the greater [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York") area. With 10 kW of broadcasting power, WRPI maintains a stronger signal than nearly all college radio stations and some commercial stations. WRPI currently broadcasts on 91.5 FM in the Albany area.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/RPIPlayhouse2.jpg/250px-RPIPlayhouse2.jpg)](/wiki/File:RPIPlayhouse2.jpg)

The RPI Playhouse

The RPI Players is an on‑campus theater group that was formed in 1929. The Players resided in the Old Gym until 1965 when they moved to their present location at the 15th Street Lounge. This distinctive red shingled building had been a USO hall for the [U.S. Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") before being purchased by RPI. However, the building is currently closed to the public due to flood damage caused by a burst pipe in February 2023.[[118]](#cite_note-118) The Players have staged over 300 productions in its history.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

### RPI songs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: RPI songs")]

Main article: [RPI songs](/wiki/RPI_songs "RPI songs")

There are a number of songs commonly played and sung at RPI events.[[120]](#cite_note-songs-120)[[121]](#cite_note-stutt_songs-121) Notable among them are:

* "The Alma Mater (Here's to Old RPI)" – sung at formal events such as [commencement](/wiki/Graduation "Graduation") and [convocation](/wiki/Convocation "Convocation"), also played and sung by the [Pep Band](/wiki/Pep_band "Pep band") at hockey and football games, and played daily at noon by the quadrangle bell tower. It was published in the first book of *Songs of Rensselaer* printed in 1913.
* "Hail, Dear Old Rensselaer" – used to be the [fight song](/wiki/Fight_song "Fight song") during the 1960s. It is still played today by the Pep Band at athletic events.
* "All We've Learned at Rensselaer" – sung at the RPI commencement ceremonies by the Rensselyrics. Although the Rensselyrics are an *[a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella")* group, this song is accompanied by piano. Each verse or section has a different musical style, several of which are closely based on [Billy Joel](/wiki/Billy_Joel "Billy Joel") songs or other popular songs.

### First Year Experience and CLASS programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: First Year Experience and CLASS programs")]

Another notable aspect of student life at RPI is the "[First-Year Experience](/wiki/First-Year_Experience "First-Year Experience")" (FYE) program. Freshman begin their stay at RPI with a week called "Navigating Rensselaer and Beyond" or NRB week. The Office of the First-Year Experience provides several programs that extend to not only freshman, but to all students. These include family weekend, community service days, the Information and Personal Assistance Center (IPAC), and the Community Advocate Program.[[122]](#cite_note-122) The FYE program was awarded the 2006 [NASPA](/wiki/National_Association_of_Student_Personnel_Administrators "National Association of Student Personnel Administrators") Excellence Gold Award, in the category of "Enrollment Management, Orientation, Parents, First-Year, Other-Year and related".[[123]](#cite_note-123)

Starting in 2008, the Division of Student Life updated the structure of its residential college model based upon the concept of "Clustered Learning Advocacy and Support for Students" (CLASS), which included a planned requirement for all sophomores to [live on campus](/wiki/Dormitory "Dormitory") and to live with special "residence cluster deans".[[124]](#cite_note-124)
The transition to this program began in early 2010 among some resistance from some fraternities and students who had planned to live off campus.[[125]](#cite_note-125)[[126]](#cite_note-126)

### NROTC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: NROTC")]

RPI [Naval Reserve Officers Training Corps](/wiki/Naval_Reserve_Officers_Training_Corps "Naval Reserve Officers Training Corps") is an officer accession program hosted at RPI with the goal of developing [Midshipmen](/wiki/Midshipmen "Midshipmen") into commissioned officers into the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") and [Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps"). The unit consists of students from RPI as well as [Union College](/wiki/Union_College "Union College"). The program was officially started at RPI in September 1941, just a few months before the US involvement in [WWII](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[127]](#cite_note-:0-127) RPI NROTC was part of the [V-12 training program](/wiki/V-12_Navy_College_Training_Program "V-12 Navy College Training Program") that was aimed at increasing the number of total commissioned officers during WWII. It focused on developing officers for the military specializing in technical degrees such as [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering"), [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine"), and foreign languages. The RPI class of 1945 had a large majority of its student body in the NROTC program with around 70% of the 932 students.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

Since 1926, over 75 Naval Officers have attained flag officer rank with a degree from RPI. Besides the [US Naval Academy](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Academy "United States Naval Academy"), this is the largest number of flag officers produced from one single institute. RPI NROTC is home to several notable alumni including NASA Astronaut [CDR Reid Wiseman](/wiki/Gregory_R._Wiseman "Gregory R. Wiseman") and [RDML Lewis Combs](/wiki/Lewis_Combs "Lewis Combs").[[127]](#cite_note-:0-127) RDML Combs is the founder of the Navy Construction Battalion, commonly referred to as the "[Seabees](/wiki/Seabee "Seabee")," which plays a crucial role in creating forward deployed bases as well as [humanitarian](/wiki/Humanitarianism "Humanitarianism") efforts to bring fresh water to underdeveloped communities.

### Grand Marshal and President of the Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Grand Marshal and President of the Union")]

Main article: [Grand Marshal (RPI)](/wiki/Grand_Marshal_(RPI) "Grand Marshal (RPI)")

The positions of [Grand Marshal](/wiki/Grand_Marshal_(RPI) "Grand Marshal (RPI)") and President of the Union at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute are typically awarded (by student vote) to the students who are respected by the student body and are represented by a top hat and a derby hat respectively. While Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute has had the position of Grand Marshal (GM) since 1865, the position of President of the Union did not come about until 1890 when the union was developed. However, between 1890 and 1894, there was only a President of the Union, no Grand Marshal. Starting with the 1894–1895 school year, the President of the Union and Grand Marshal ran side by side. The week of voting for said positions is called GM Week, and typically has events on campus for students to do to promote the voting.

The Grand Marshal position is elected by the student body (typically in the spring) and is the highest position a student can hold at RPI. The GM plays a big role in the student government by doing projects, appointing officers, and aiding the general campus community.[[129]](#cite_note-129) The first GM was Albert M. Harper.[[130]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-130)

The President of the Union is elected by the student body (typically in the spring). Their main responsibility is to appoint an executive board of 15–20 students (responsible for the Rensselaer Union's budget). The executive board is the chief financial body of the Rensselaer Union. They are responsible for preparing and approving the budget for the following fiscal year, keeping track of and distributing union funds, and managing the business affairs with other facilities. In addition to appointing the executive board, they also work closely with the union's staff, athletics staff, and student clubs.[[131]](#cite_note-131) The first PU was W.C.H. Slagle.[[130]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-130)

### Greek life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Greek life")]

Main article: [List of RPI fraternities and sororities](/wiki/List_of_RPI_fraternities_and_sororities "List of RPI fraternities and sororities")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Theta_Chi_Phi.jpg/250px-Theta_Chi_Phi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Theta_Chi_Phi.jpg)

The Theta Chapter of [Chi Phi](/wiki/Chi_Phi "Chi Phi") at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Rensselaer_Society_of_Engineers_clubhouse_%28rear_view%29.jpg/250px-Rensselaer_Society_of_Engineers_clubhouse_%28rear_view%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rensselaer_Society_of_Engineers_clubhouse_(rear_view).jpg)

The [RSE](/wiki/Rensselaer_Society_of_Engineers "Rensselaer Society of Engineers") Clubhouse from the South Side near the "Freshman Hill" dorms

Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute has an extensive history of Greek community involvement on campus, including past presidents, honorary academic building dedications, and [philanthropic](/wiki/Philanthropy "Philanthropy") achievements. The overall Greek system at Rensselaer stresses Leadership, Fortitude, Innovation, and Evolution. RPI currently has 29 active fraternities as well as 6 sororities, with 32 percent involvement of all males and 18 percent involvement of all females, organized under the [Interfraternal Council](/wiki/North_American_Interfraternity_Conference "North American Interfraternity Conference") and [Panhellenic Council](/wiki/National_Panhellenic_Conference "National Panhellenic Conference").[[132]](#cite_note-132) Of those Greek organizations, three were founded at Rensselaer including the [Theta Xi](/wiki/Theta_Xi "Theta Xi") national fraternity, the [Sigma Delta](/wiki/Sigma_Delta "Sigma Delta") Hispanic-interest local sorority, and the [Rensselaer Society of Engineers](/wiki/Rensselaer_Society_of_Engineers "Rensselaer Society of Engineers") local engineering fraternity. [Theta Xi](/wiki/Theta_Xi "Theta Xi") fraternity was established by RPI students on 29 April 1864, the only national fraternity founded during the Civil War. The [Theta Xi Fraternity Chapter House](/wiki/Theta_Xi_Fraternity_Chapter_House "Theta Xi Fraternity Chapter House") is listed on the National Register of Historic Places. Additionally, Rensselaer is home to the [Epsilon Zeta](/wiki/Epsilon_Eta "Epsilon Eta") chapter of the [Alpha Phi Omega](/wiki/Alpha_Phi_Omega "Alpha Phi Omega"), or "APO," national service fraternity, which operates a test-bank and office at the top floor of the Student Union. The organization also hosts a campus lost & found, universal can tab collection, and a public 3D printing service.

In 2017, [Chi Phi](/wiki/Chi_Phi "Chi Phi") and [Theta Chi](/wiki/Theta_Chi "Theta Chi") at Rensselaer co-hosted an event called "Brave A Shave For Kids With Cancer," along with several other Greek organizations - raising over $22,000 for pediatric cancer research with dozens of participants shaving their heads to spread awareness of pediatric cancers. Many fraternities and sororities also engage in [Adopt-a-Highway](/wiki/Adopt-a-Highway "Adopt-a-Highway") and host events in the local community.[[133]](#cite_note-133) Since its inception, all members of Greek Life have also participated in Navigating Rensselaer & Beyond - RPI's official continuation of student orientation through hosting annual events open to all students such as Beach Day/Hike with Greek Life, a day of hiking and team building activities for incoming freshmen, and Saratoga Therapeutic Equine Program, a day of service focused on horse rehabilitation programs.

Greek Life organizations also operate Greek-affiliated groups including the Alumni Inter-Greek Council, Greek Greeks - a student-run venture which aims to promote sustainability and safe environmental practices in Greek chapter houses, Greek Spectrum - an [LGBTQIA](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") support and advocacy group, and the undergraduate Greek leadership society [Order of Omega](/wiki/Order_of_Omega "Order of Omega").

## Student body

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Student body")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/RPIQuad.jpg/960px-RPIQuad.jpg)](/wiki/File:RPIQuad.jpg "Rensselaer's Quadrangle dormitory on the central campus")

Rensselaer's Quadrangle dormitory on the central campus

In 2018, Rensselaer's enrollment was 7,442 total resident students, including 6,590 undergraduate and 1,329 graduate.[[134]](#cite_note-President's_Report_2018-134) Over 71% of Rensselaer's students are from out of state. More than 20% of students are international. Rensselaer students represent all 50 U.S. states and over 60 countries. The undergraduate student to faculty ratio is 13:1. Among the class of 2020, 66% are in the top 5 percent of their high school class, 93% in the top quarter, and 99% in the top half. The average unweighted high school GPA for enrolled students was 3.88 on a 4.0 scale, with 65% having a 3.75 GPA or higher and 99% having at least a 3.0.[[135]](#cite_note-135)

Rensselaer's yield rate for the Class of 2021 surpassed 20 percent in the year 2018 with over 20,000 applications received by Rensselaer's Office of Admissions.[[136]](#cite_note-President's_Report_2016-136) The average SAT score range was 1330–1500 for the mid-50% range with a median SAT score of 1420 on a scale of 1600. The average ACT score range was 29–33 for the mid-50% range with a median ACT score of 31.[[137]](#cite_note-Facts_&_Figures-137) In 2016, Rensselaer's freshman retention rate was 94% and admissions selectivity rating was 35th in the nation according to *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*. Since 2000, undergraduate enrollment grew by over 1,700 students, from 4,867 to 6,590 during calendar year 2018, and the full-time graduate enrollment declined from 1500 to 1,188.[[74]](#cite_note-Renew_Rensselaer-74)[[138]](#cite_note-Renew_Rensselaer_rev-138)

Roughly 12% of students received the Rensselaer medal, a merit scholarship with a cumulative value of $100,000 for exceptional high school students in science and mathematics.[[137]](#cite_note-Facts_&_Figures-137) 95% of full-time domestic undergraduate students receive either need-based or merit-based [financial aid](/wiki/Student_financial_aid "Student financial aid"), averaging 85% of total financial need met per student.[[139]](#cite_note-139)[[140]](#cite_note-financial_aid-140) In 2018, Rensselaer invested over $140 million in financial aid and scholarships for students.[[140]](#cite_note-financial_aid-140)

### Gender ratio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Gender ratio")]

RPI became coeducational in 1942. In 1966, the male-to-female ratio was 19:1, in the 1980s it reached as low as 8:1, and in the early 1990s the ratio was around 5:1. In 2009, RPI had a ratio of 2.5:1 (72% male / 28% female),[[141]](#cite_note-Common_Data_Set-141)[[142]](#cite_note-142) In 2016, the ratio for the incoming freshman class had fallen to 2.1:1 (68% male / 32% female), the lowest in the history of the institute.[[143]](#cite_note-Inside_Rensselaer_Magazine-143) In the fall of 2016, more than 1,000 women enrolled in Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute's undergraduate engineering programs for the first time in its history. These women represented 30 percent of the student body in engineering at the university, and 32 percent of the university's total gender composition. Shekhar Garde, Rensselaer's dean of engineering, claims he wants to increase the female composition of the institute to 50 percent before 2030.[[144]](#cite_note-144)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main articles: [RPI Engineers](/wiki/RPI_Engineers "RPI Engineers"), [RPI Engineers men's ice hockey](/wiki/RPI_Engineers_men%27s_ice_hockey "RPI Engineers men's ice hockey"), and [RPI Engineers women's ice hockey](/wiki/RPI_Engineers_women%27s_ice_hockey "RPI Engineers women's ice hockey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/RPI_Engineers.svg/250px-RPI_Engineers.svg.png)](/wiki/File:RPI_Engineers.svg)

Official athletics logo

The **RPI Engineers** are the athletic teams for the university. RPI currently sponsors 23 sports, 21 of which compete at the [NCAA Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") level in the [Liberty League](/wiki/Liberty_League "Liberty League"); men's and women's [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") compete at the [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") level in [ECAC Hockey](/wiki/ECAC_Hockey "ECAC Hockey"). The official nickname of some of the school's Division III teams was changed in 1995 from the Engineers to the Red Hawks. However, the hockey, football, cross-country, tennis and track and field teams all chose to retain the Engineers name. The Red Hawks name was, at the time, very unpopular among the student body; a Red Hawk mascot was frequently taunted with thrown concessions and chants of "kill the chicken!". In 2009 the nickname for all teams has since been changed back to Engineers. In contrast, the official ice hockey mascot, known as [Puckman](/wiki/Puck_man "Puck man"), has always been very popular. Puckman is an [anthropomorphic](/wiki/Anthropomorphism "Anthropomorphism") hockey puck with an engineer's helmet.

During the 1970s and 1980s, one RPI cheer was:

:   *E to the x, dy/dx, E to the x, dx*
:   *Cosine, secant, tangent, sine*
:   *3.14159*
:   *Square root, cube root, log of pi*
:   *Disintegrate them, RPI!*[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146)

### Ice hockey (men's)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Ice hockey (men's)")]

Main article: [RPI Engineers men's ice hockey](/wiki/RPI_Engineers_men%27s_ice_hockey "RPI Engineers men's ice hockey")

RPI has a competitive Division I [hockey team](/wiki/RPI_Engineers_men%27s_ice_hockey "RPI Engineers men's ice hockey") who won [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") national titles in 1954 and 1985. Depending on how the rules are interpreted, the RPI men's ice hockey team may have the longest winning streak on record for a Division I team; in the 1984–85 season it was undefeated for 30 games, but one game was against the [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto"), a non-NCAA team. Continuing into the 1985–86 season, RPI continued undefeated over 38 games, including two wins over Toronto.[[147]](#cite_note-147) The streak ended at [Boston University](/wiki/Boston_University "Boston University") against the [Terriers](/wiki/Boston_University_Terriers_men%27s_ice_hockey "Boston University Terriers men's ice hockey"). [Adam Oates](/wiki/Adam_Oates "Adam Oates") and [Daren Puppa](/wiki/Daren_Puppa "Daren Puppa"), two players during that time, both went on to become stars in the [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"). [Joé Juneau](/wiki/Jo%C3%A9_Juneau "Joé Juneau"), who played from 1987 to 1991, and [Brian Pothier](/wiki/Brian_Pothier "Brian Pothier"), who played from 1996 to 2000, also spent many years in the NHL. [Graeme Townshend](/wiki/Graeme_Townshend "Graeme Townshend"), who also played in the late 1980s, had a brief NHL career. He is the first man of [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaicans "Jamaicans") ancestry to play in the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League").

The ice hockey team plays a significant role in the campus's culture, drawing thousands of fans each week to the [Houston Field House](/wiki/Houston_Field_House "Houston Field House") during the season. The team's popularity even sparked the tradition of the hockey line, where students lined up for season tickets months in advance of the on-sale date. Today, the line generally begins a week or more before ticket sales.[[148]](#cite_note-148) Another tradition since 1978 has been the "Big Red Freakout!" game held close to the first weekend of February. Fans usually dress in the school's colors red (cherry) and white, and gifts such as T-shirts are distributed en masse. In ice hockey, the RPI's biggest rival has always been the upstate engineering school [Clarkson University](/wiki/Clarkson_University "Clarkson University"). In recent years RPI has also developed a spirited rivalry with their conference travel partner [Union College](/wiki/Union_College "Union College"), with whom they annually play a nonconference game in [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York") for the Mayor's Cup.

### Ice hockey (women's)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Ice hockey (women's)")]

Main article: [RPI Engineers women's ice hockey](/wiki/RPI_Engineers_women%27s_ice_hockey "RPI Engineers women's ice hockey")

The women's ice hockey team moved to the NCAA Division I level in 2005. During the 2008–09 season the team set the record for most wins in one season (19-14-4). On 28 February 2010, Rensselaer made NCAA history. The Engineers beat [Quinnipiac](/wiki/Quinnipiac_Bobcats_women%27s_ice_hockey "Quinnipiac Bobcats women's ice hockey"), 2–1, but it took five overtimes. It is now the longest game in [NCAA Women's Ice Hockey](/wiki/NCAA_women%27s_ice_hockey_tournament "NCAA women's ice hockey tournament") history. Senior defenseman Laura Gersten had the game-winning goal. She registered it at 4:32 of the fifth overtime session to not only clinch the win, but the series victory.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Lacrosse_at_the_Olympics%2C_London%2C_1948._%287649951098%29.jpg/250px-Lacrosse_at_the_Olympics%2C_London%2C_1948._%287649951098%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lacrosse_at_the_Olympics,_London,_1948._(7649951098).jpg)

RPI's lacrosse team in a [demonstration game](/wiki/Demonstration_sport "Demonstration sport") at the [1948 London Olympics](/wiki/1948_Summer_Olympics "1948 Summer Olympics")

### Lacrosse (men's)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Lacrosse (men's)")]

The [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse") team represented the United States in the [1948 Olympics](/wiki/Lacrosse_at_the_1948_Summer_Olympics "Lacrosse at the 1948 Summer Olympics") in London. It won the [Wingate Memorial Trophy](/wiki/Wingate_Memorial_Trophy "Wingate Memorial Trophy") as national collegiate champions in 1952.[[150]](#cite_note-150) Future [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL") head coach [Ned Harkness](/wiki/Ned_Harkness "Ned Harkness") coached the lacrosse and ice hockey teams, winning national championships in both sports.

### Baseball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Baseball")]

The Engineers baseball squad is perennially atop the [Liberty League](/wiki/Liberty_League "Liberty League") standings and has seen 8 players move on to the professional ranks, including 4 players selected in the [MLB](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") draft. The team is coached by Jason Falcon. The Engineers play their home games at the historic Robison Field.

### American football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: American football")]

[American rugby](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_the_United_States "Rugby union in the United States") was played on campus in the late 1870s. [Intercollegiate football](/wiki/Intercollegiate_football "Intercollegiate football") begin as late as 1886 when an RPI team first played a [Union College](/wiki/Union_College "Union College") team on a leased field in West Troy ([Watervliet](/wiki/Watervliet,_New_York "Watervliet, New York")). Since 1903, RPI and nearby Union have been rivals in football, making it the oldest such rivalry in the state. The teams have played for the [Dutchman's Shoes](/wiki/Dutchman%27s_Shoes "Dutchman's Shoes") since 1950. RPI Football had their most successful season in 2003, when they finished 11–2 and lost to [St. Johns](/wiki/College_of_Saint_Benedict_and_Saint_John%27s_University "College of Saint Benedict and Saint John's University") (Minn.) in the NCAA Division III semifinal game.[[151]](#cite_note-151)

### Athletic facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Athletic facilities")]

See also: [Houston Field House](/wiki/Houston_Field_House "Houston Field House")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/RPIHoustonFieldHouse.jpg/250px-RPIHoustonFieldHouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:RPIHoustonFieldHouse.jpg)

Houston Field House

The [Houston Field House](/wiki/Houston_Field_House "Houston Field House") is a 4,780‑seat multi-purpose arena located on the RPI campus. It opened in 1949 and is home to the RPI Engineers men's and women's ice hockey teams. The Field House was renovated starting in 2007 as part of the major campus improvement project to build the East Campus Athletic Village. The renovations included locker rooms upgrades, addition of a new weight room, and a new special reception room dedicated to [Ned Harkness](/wiki/Ned_Harkness "Ned Harkness").[[152]](#cite_note-152) Additionally, as part of the renovations through a government grant, [solar panels](/wiki/Solar_panel "Solar panel") were installed on the roof to supply power to the building.

As part of the Rensselaer Plan, the institute recently completed a major project to improve its athletic facilities with the East Campus Athletic Village. The plan included construction of a new and much larger 4,842‑seat football stadium, a basketball arena with seating for 1,200, a new 50-meter pool, an indoor track and field complex, new tennis courts, new weight rooms and a new sports medicine center.[[153]](#cite_note-153) The institute broke ground on 26 August 2007, and construction of the first phase is expected to last two years.[[154]](#cite_note-154) The estimated cost of the project is $78 million for phase one and $35–$45 million for phase two.[[155]](#cite_note-155) Since the completion of the new stadium, the bleachers on the Class of '86 football field on the central campus have been removed and the field has become an open space. In the future the new space could be used for expansions of the academic buildings, but for now members of the campus planning team foresee a "historic landscape with different paths and access ways for students and vehicles alike".[[156]](#cite_note-156)

## History of women

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: History of women")]

Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute has historically been a male dominated institute. The first woman to apply to RPI applied in 1873, and her name was Elizabeth R. Bruswell. However, she did not attend as she was the only female to apply, and it was suggested that she would not be accepted as it would not be comfortable for her as the only woman on campus. For many years afterwards, the school continued to only allow admission to men. It wasn't until 1942 that women were welcome to enroll in classes at Rensselaer.

### First women

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: First women")]

#### Students

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Students")]

Camilla (Trent) Cluett (Architecture), Elizabeth English (Biology), Helen Ketchum (Architecture), [Lois Graham](/wiki/Lois_Graham "Lois Graham") (Mechanical Engineering), and Mary Ellen Rathbun (Metallurgical Engineering) were the first women to enroll in 1942. Lois Graham and Mary Ellen Rathbun became the first to graduate on April 22, 1945.[[157]](#cite_note-archives.rpi.edu-157) In addition, Antoinette A. Patti was the first woman to receive a master's degree from RPI in February 1947, in Chemistry. The first Doctoral Degree received by a woman at RPI was Reva R. G. Servoss in June 1954, in Chemistry as well.

#### Faculty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Faculty")]

Miss Hazel Brennan was the first woman assistant instructor in chemistry in 1918 and was officially the first woman instructor. The following year, 1919, Marie De Pierpont was hired as an instructor in French and was later named professor as well as being named head of the language department in 1928. She was the first woman to hold a full professorship at the institute and ended her position in 1932. It wasn't for another 11 years (1943) that another woman was hired as an instructor. [Herta Leng](/wiki/Herta_Regina_Leng "Herta Regina Leng"), who worked in the Physics Department, was given the title of assistant professor in 1945, and became a full professor in 1966, the second woman to be a full professor at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute.[[157]](#cite_note-archives.rpi.edu-157)

While the percentage of women enrolled at RPI remains comparatively low, it has improved significantly over time. The ratio as of 2020 was about 32% women and 68% men.[[158]](#cite_note-158)

## Notable alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Notable alumni")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute people](/wiki/List_of_Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute_people "List of Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute people").

According to the Rensselaer Alumni Association, there are nearly 100,000 RPI graduates living in the United States, and another 4,378 living abroad.[[159]](#cite_note-159) In 1995, the Alumni Association created the Rensselaer Alumni Hall of Fame.[[160]](#cite_note-AHOF-160)

Several notable 19th-century civil engineers graduated from RPI. These include the visionary of the transcontinental railroad, [Theodore Judah](/wiki/Theodore_Judah "Theodore Judah"), [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge") engineer [Washington Roebling](/wiki/Washington_Roebling "Washington Roebling"), [George Washington Gale Ferris Jr.](/wiki/George_Washington_Gale_Ferris_Jr. "George Washington Gale Ferris Jr.") (who designed and built the original [Ferris Wheel](/wiki/Ferris_Wheel_(1893) "Ferris Wheel (1893)")) and [Leffert L. Buck](/wiki/Leffert_L._Buck "Leffert L. Buck"), the chief engineer of the [Williamsburg Bridge](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge") in New York City.[[160]](#cite_note-AHOF-160)

Many RPI graduates have made important inventions, including [Allen B. DuMont](/wiki/Allen_B._DuMont "Allen B. DuMont") ('24),[[161]](#cite_note-161) creator of the first commercial [television](/wiki/Television "Television") and [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar"); [Keith D. Millis](/wiki/Keith_Millis "Keith Millis") ('38),[[162]](#cite_note-162) inventor of [ductile iron](/wiki/Ductile_iron "Ductile iron"); [Ted Hoff](/wiki/Marcian_Hoff "Marcian Hoff") ('58),[[163]](#cite_note-163) father of the [microprocessor](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor"); [Raymond Tomlinson](/wiki/Raymond_Tomlinson "Raymond Tomlinson") ('63),[[164]](#cite_note-164) often credited with the invention of [e-mail](/wiki/Email "Email"); inventor of the [digital camera](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera") [Steven Sasson](/wiki/Steven_Sasson "Steven Sasson")[[165]](#cite_note-The_Rediff_Interview/Steven_J_Sasson,_inventor_of_the_digital_camera-165) and [Curtis Priem](/wiki/Curtis_Priem "Curtis Priem") ('82), designer of the first [graphics processor](/wiki/Graphics_processing_unit "Graphics processing unit") for the PC, and co-founder of [NVIDIA](/wiki/NVIDIA "NVIDIA"). RPI Prof. [Matthew Hunter](/wiki/Matthew_Hunter "Matthew Hunter") invented a process to refine [titanium](/wiki/Titanium "Titanium") in 1910. [H. Joseph Gerber](/wiki/H._Joseph_Gerber "H. Joseph Gerber") pioneered computer-automated manufacturing systems for industry.

In addition to NVIDIA, RPI graduates have also gone on to found or co-found major companies such as [John Wiley and Sons](/wiki/John_Wiley_and_Sons "John Wiley and Sons"), [Texas Instruments](/wiki/Texas_Instruments "Texas Instruments"), [Fairchild Semiconductor](/wiki/Fairchild_Semiconductor "Fairchild Semiconductor"), [PSINet](/wiki/PSINet "PSINet"), [MapInfo](/wiki/MapInfo_Corporation "MapInfo Corporation"), [Adelphia Communications](/wiki/Adelphia_Communications "Adelphia Communications"), [Level 3 Communications](/wiki/Level_3_Communications "Level 3 Communications"), [Garmin](/wiki/Garmin "Garmin"), [Bugle Boy](/wiki/Bugle_Boy "Bugle Boy"), [Vacasa](/wiki/Vacasa "Vacasa"), and [Rivian](/wiki/Rivian "Rivian"). Several RPI graduates have played a part in the [U.S. space program](/wiki/NASA "NASA"): [George Low](/wiki/George_Low "George Low") (B.Eng. 1948, M.S. 1950) was manager of the [Apollo 11 project](/wiki/Apollo_program "Apollo program") and served as president of RPI, and astronauts [John L. Swigert Jr.](/wiki/John_Swigert "John Swigert"), [Richard Mastracchio](/wiki/Richard_Mastracchio "Richard Mastracchio"), [Gregory R. Wiseman](/wiki/Gregory_R._Wiseman "Gregory R. Wiseman"), and space tourist [Dennis Tito](/wiki/Dennis_Tito "Dennis Tito") are alumni. The [Electric Power Research Institute](/wiki/Electric_Power_Research_Institute "Electric Power Research Institute") (EPRI) was founded by Dr. [Chauncey Starr](/wiki/Chauncey_Starr "Chauncey Starr") who graduated from RPI with a PhD in physics in 1935.

Political figures who graduated from RPI included [federal judge](/wiki/United_States_federal_judge "United States federal judge") [Arthur J. Gajarsa](/wiki/Arthur_J._Gajarsa "Arthur J. Gajarsa") (B.S. 1962), and Major General [Thomas Farrell](/wiki/Thomas_Farrell_(general) "Thomas Farrell (general)") of the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project"). [Edward Burton Hughes](/wiki/Edward_Burton_Hughes "Edward Burton Hughes"), the Acting Commissioner of [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") in 1969, Executive Deputy Commissioner of [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") from 1967 to 1970, and Deputy Superintendent of [New York State Department of Public Works](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Public_Works "New York State Department of Public Works") from 1952 to 1967. [Bertram Dalley Tallamy](/wiki/Bertram_Dalley_Tallamy "Bertram Dalley Tallamy"), seventh Federal Highway Administrator under the Federal Aid Highway Act of 1956, in office from February 5, 1957 – January 20, 1961. [DARPA](/wiki/DARPA "DARPA") director [Tony Tether](/wiki/Tony_Tether "Tony Tether"), Representative [John Olver](/wiki/John_Olver "John Olver") of [Massachusetts's 1st congressional district](/wiki/Massachusetts%27s_1st_congressional_district "Massachusetts's 1st congressional district"), and Senators [John Barrasso](/wiki/John_Barrasso "John Barrasso") of [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming"), [Mark Shepard](/wiki/Mark_Shepard "Mark Shepard") of [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"), and [George R. Dennis](/wiki/George_R._Dennis "George R. Dennis") of [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland"), [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_minister "Prime minister") [Hani Al-Mulki](/wiki/Hani_Al-Mulki "Hani Al-Mulki") of [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan").

Notable ice hockey players include [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") [Hockey Hall of Famer](/wiki/Hockey_Hall_of_Fame "Hockey Hall of Fame") and five-time NHL [All Star](/wiki/All-star "All-star") [Adam Oates](/wiki/Adam_Oates "Adam Oates") (1985), [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") winner and former NHL All Star [Mike McPhee](/wiki/Mike_McPhee "Mike McPhee") (1982), two-time [Calder Cup](/wiki/Calder_Cup "Calder Cup") winner [Neil Little](/wiki/Neil_Little "Neil Little") (1994), former NHL All Rookie [Joé Juneau](/wiki/Jo%C3%A9_Juneau "Joé Juneau") (1991), and former NHL All Star [Daren Puppa](/wiki/Daren_Puppa "Daren Puppa") (1985).

Other notable alumni include 1973 [Nobel Prize in Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics") winner [Ivar Giaever](/wiki/Ivar_Giaever "Ivar Giaever") (Ph.D. 1964);[[166]](#cite_note-166) the first African American woman to become a thoracic surgeon, [Rosalyn Scott](/wiki/Rosalyn_Scott "Rosalyn Scott") (B.S. 1970); director of Linux International [Jon Hall](/wiki/Jon_Hall_(programmer) "Jon Hall (programmer)") (M.S. 1977); [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") president [Myles Brand](/wiki/Myles_Brand "Myles Brand") (B.S. 1964);[[167]](#cite_note-167) [Lois Graham](/wiki/Lois_Graham "Lois Graham") (B.S.ME 1946), who was the first woman to receive a degree in engineering from RPI, and went on to become the first woman in the US to receive a PhD in engineering; adult stem cell pioneer [James Fallon](/wiki/James_H._Fallon "James H. Fallon"); [Michael D. West](/wiki/Michael_D._West "Michael D. West"), [gerontologist](/wiki/Gerontology "Gerontology") and [stem cell](/wiki/Stem_cell "Stem cell") scientist, founder of [Geron](/wiki/Geron_Corporation "Geron Corporation"), now CEO of [BioTime](/wiki/BioTime "BioTime") (1976); director [Bobby Farrelly](/wiki/Bobby_Farrelly "Bobby Farrelly") (1981),[[168]](#cite_note-168) [David Ferrucci](/wiki/David_Ferrucci "David Ferrucci"), lead researcher on IBM's [Watson](/wiki/Watson_(computer) "Watson (computer)")/Jeopardy! project; 66th AIA Gold Medal-winning architect Peter Q Bohlin; [Matt Patricia](/wiki/Matt_Patricia "Matt Patricia"), former head coach for the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions"); [Luis Acuña (Politician)](/w/index.php?title=Luis_Acu%C3%B1a_(Politician)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luis Acuña (Politician) (page does not exist)") [[es](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Luis_Acu%C3%B1a_(pol%C3%ADtico) "es:Luis Acuña (político)")], Governor of the Venezuelan Sucre State and former Minister of Universities; [Andrew Franks](/wiki/Andrew_Franks "Andrew Franks"), former placekicker for the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League"); [Sean Conroy](/wiki/Sean_Conroy "Sean Conroy"), the first openly gay professional baseball player; [Prem Jain](/wiki/Prem_Jain "Prem Jain") (Father of Green Buildings in India); [Keith Raniere](/wiki/Keith_Raniere "Keith Raniere"), an [American](/wiki/United_States "United States") [felon](/wiki/Felony "Felony") and the founder of [NXIVM](/wiki/NXIVM "NXIVM"), a [multi-level marketing](/wiki/Multi-level_marketing "Multi-level marketing") company and [cult](/wiki/Cult "Cult").[[169]](#cite_note-169)
